title: Chapter One: The Red Dust
pages:
Dawn on Mars was a lie.
The sun rose over the eastern ridge of Valles Marineris like a pale,
anemic ghost, casting long shadows across the rust-colored wasteland.
Detective Jax gripped the steering yoke of her battered police spinner,
watching the red dust spiral up from the treads as she pushed the
vehicle hard across the ancient lakebed. The dust didn’t just coat the
windshield—it coated everything. The boots, the lungs, the soul. It
tasted like old iron and deferred dreams, and it never, ever washed out.
She’d been driving for forty minutes, leaving the cramped, subter-
ranean warrens of Sector 4 behind and climbing toward the Zenith
Domes. The domes rose on the horizon like crystalline tumors, gleaming
and obscene against the dead Martian sky. Up there, the air was clean.
The gravity was Earth-normal. The wealthy didn’t have to ration water
or worry about structural failures in the biodome seals.
Up there, people pretended Mars was already won.

Jax knew better. She’d learned it at fourteen, buried under two hundred
meters of collapsed rock in the Tantalus Mining Camp, listening to her
parents’ final, wet breaths in the darkness. Mars didn’t forgive. It didn’t
compromise. And the people who profited from its conquest were the
same ones who cut corners on the safety equipment that killed miners
by the dozen.
She hated the Zenith Domes. But a body was a body, and the dead
didn’t care about her feelings.
The red dust of Ares Prime didn’t just coat the boots; it coated the
soul. It crept through the micro-fractures in the lower-level biodomes,
settled in the lungs, and tasted like old iron and deferred dreams.
Detective Jax hated the dust. But standing in the pristine, climate-
controlled foyer of Penthouse 4A in the Zenith Domes, she found herself
missing it. Up here, the air tasted like nothing. It was scrubbed so clean
of impurities, heavily oxygenated, and laced with a faint, synthetic hint
of pine, that it made her teeth ache.
The Zenith Domes weren’t just a different neighborhood—they were
a different planet. Built on reinforced bedrock with localized gravity
plating that mimicked Earth-standard pull, the domes housed the
colonial elite in a bubble of artificial perfection. The air pressure was
regulated to sea-level equivalent. The water wasn’t recycled gray sludge
but actual, distilled purity piped in from the northern ice caps. The floors
were imported marble. The lighting was soft, warm, and designed by
psychologists to reduce stress and promote productivity.
It was suffocating.
Jax had worked homicides in the lower sectors for fifteen years. She
knew what murder looked like when it was born from desperation—a
water ration dispute that ended with a shiv between the ribs, a gang
execution in a rusted-out hab module, a domestic violence call that
went sideways when the husband’s fists finally did what they’d been
threatening for months. Those crime scenes smelled like sweat and fear

and the coppery tang of blood pooling on cheap polymer flooring.
This was different. Up here, murder wore a tailored suit. It had a
corporate expense account. It happened in penthouses with vaulted
ceilings and smart-glass windows that cost more than Jax would earn in
a decade. The violence was cleaner, more precise, but no less brutal. If
anything, it was worse—because up here, the killers had the resources
to make the bodies disappear, to buy silence, to ensure that justice was a
commodity reserved for those who could afford it.
Jax felt the familiar tightness in her chest, the claustrophobic weight
of being somewhere she didn’t belong. The air was too clean. The
silence was too perfect. Even the hum of the environmental systems was
engineered to be soothing, a low, almost subsonic vibration that was
supposed to calm the nervous system.
It made her want to punch something.
Jax stepped off the mag-lev elevator, her worn, synth-leather trench
coat starkly out of place against the gleaming white marble of the
corridor. The Zenith Domes hovered in the upper stratosphere of
the colony, reserved exclusively for the billionaires who owned the
terraforming rigs, the water rations, and the politicians.
“Detective on deck,” a uniformed transit cop muttered, stepping back
from the crime scene tape.
Jax didn’t acknowledge him. She ducked under the glowing yellow
holo-tape and stepped into the penthouse. The sheer scale of the room
was a vulgar display of wealth. Vaulted ceilings, expansive glass walls
looking out over the sprawling, neon-scarred expanse of the lower
colony, and gravity-dampeners that made every step feel unnervingly
light.
The penthouse stretched out before her like an architectural fever
dream. The main living area was easily two hundred square meters, with
a sunken seating arrangement centered around a holographic fireplace
that crackled with simulated flames. The furniture was all organic

materials—real wood, real leather, the kind of luxury that required
shipping costs measured in the tens of thousands of credits per kilogram.
Abstract art hung on the walls, the kind of incomprehensible splashes of
color that the wealthy pretended to understand. A full bar dominated
the western wall, stocked with bottles of actual, Earth-distilled alcohol
that would cost a lower-sector worker a month’s salary per shot.
But beneath the opulence, Jax’s trained eye caught the details that
mattered. No signs of forced entry on the reinforced security door.
No scuff marks on the polished floor leading from the entrance. The
smart-home system was still active, playing a low, classical symphony—
something baroque, maybe Bach—that felt profoundly grotesque given
the circumstances. The temperature was precisely regulated at twenty-
one degrees Celsius. The lighting was set to “evening ambient,” a warm,
golden glow that made everything look softer than it was.
Whoever had done this had been invited in. Or they’d had access codes.
Then, the smell hit her. It cut right through the artificial pine.
Burnt meat. Ozone. Copper.
The smell was wrong for the space. It didn’t belong in this sanitized,
climate-controlled bubble of wealth. It was organic, visceral, and
unmistakably human. Jax’s stomach tightened—not from nausea, but
from recognition. She’d smelled that particular combination before, in
the aftermath of an industrial accident in Sector 7 where a worker had
been caught in a plasma cutter malfunction. The ozone meant high-
energy discharge. The burnt meat meant cauterization. The copper
meant blood, even if there wasn’t much of it visible.
This wasn’t a crime of passion. This was surgical.
“Over here, Jax.”
Officer Chen was already by the primary seating area, her standard-
issue datapad casting a pale blue glow over her eager, slightly flushed
face. Chen was new to Homicide, fresh out of the upper-level academy,
and still possessed the annoying habit of looking at a corpse as a puzzle

rather than a tragedy.
Jax crossed the room, pulling a pair of sterile molecular-weave gloves
from her pockets and snapping them over her hands. “Talk to me, Chen.
What ruins my only day off in three weeks?”
“Victim is Marcus Tyrell, forty-two,” Chen said, swiping a finger
across her datapad. “Senior VP of Atmospheric Engineering for Vance
Interplanetary. Single, no registered co-habitants. The transit patrol
got an automated ping from the penthouse’s medical sensors twenty
minutes ago indicating a sudden cessation of vital signs.”
“Sudden is one word for it,” Jax murmured, stopping at the edge of a
genuine, imported-from-Earth Persian rug.
Tyrell was sprawled on his back, his eyes wide and glassy, reflecting
the cold light of the chandelier above. He was dressed in a tailored silk
lounge suit, but the front of it was completely ruined.
Jax knelt, her knees popping—a parting gift from a riot in Sector 4
three years ago. She leaned in close, careful not to let her coat brush the
fibers of the rug. There was virtually no blood, which was what made
the smell so overpowering. The victim’s chest had been laid open with
terrifying precision.
The wounds were clean. Too clean. Jax had seen her share of stabbings,
shootings, blunt-force trauma—violence was rarely neat. But this was
different. The incisions ran in parallel lines across Tyrell’s sternum and
ribcage, each cut approximately two centimeters apart. The edges of the
flesh were blackened and slightly raised, the telltale signature of instant
cauterization. Whoever had done this had taken their time. The cuts
weren’t frenzied or random—they were methodical, almost exploratory.
Jax’s mind catalogued the details automatically. No defensive wounds
on the hands or forearms. No signs of restraint marks on the wrists
or ankles. Tyrell’s expression was frozen in shock rather than agony,
which suggested the first cut had been fast enough to induce immediate
trauma. The killer had known exactly where to strike.

“Look at the margins,” Jax pointed a gloved finger at the wounds. The
edges of the flesh were blackened, instantly cauterized.
“A Class-4 laser scalpel,” Chen breathed, leaning in closer, her dark
eyes widening. “Those are military-grade. Highly restricted. You
couldn’t even get one past the lower-level security checkpoints without
tripping a dozen alarms, let alone up into the Zenith Domes.”
“Someone did,” Jax said. Her voice was flat, devoid of the shock Chen
was currently radiating. “And they didn’t just slash wildly. This isn’t a
crime of passion, Chen. This is dissection.”
She studied the pattern of the cuts more closely. The killer had opened
the chest cavity with surgical precision, exposing the heart and lungs
without damaging the surrounding tissue more than necessary. It was
the kind of work that required not just access to restricted equipment,
but training. Medical training, or military. Someone who knew anatomy,
who understood how to cut without causing unnecessary damage.
“Chen, what’s the ambient temperature reading in this room?”
Chen glanced at her datapad. “Twenty-one degrees Celsius. Standard
climate control.”
“And time of death?”
“Medical sensors logged the cessation of vitals at 0342 hours. That’s
approximately two hours ago.”
Jax nodded slowly. “So the body’s been here for two hours in a climate-
controlled environment. No decomposition, no significant blood pooling.
The killer cauterized as they cut, which means minimal blood spatter.
They were careful. Methodical.”
“Why go to all that trouble?” Chen asked, her voice dropping to a
whisper. “If you’re going to kill someone, why make it so… precise?”
“Because they were looking for something,” Jax said quietly. “Or they
were sending a message.”
Jax swept her gaze around the immediate vicinity. No signs of forced
entry. No shattered glass. No scuff marks on the pristine marble flooring

leading to the rug. The smart-home system was humming along happily,
playing a low, classical symphony that felt profoundly grotesque given
the circumstances.
“Run a localized holo-sweep for footprints or foreign micro-fibers,”
Jax ordered, standing up and rolling her shoulders to release the tension
knotting in her neck. “And get forensics up here to pull the victim’s
comms logs.”
“Already requested,” Chen said brightly. “Though Forensics said
they’re backed up with a mass transit derailment in Sector 7.”
“Tell them an executive for Vance Interplanetary just got carved up like
a Sunday roast. They’ll find the time.” Jax walked toward the massive
floor-to-ceiling smart window. She touched the glass. It was cold.
Out there, beyond the protective dome, the Martian surface was an
endless ocean of rusted rock and deep, shadowy craters. It was a dead
world that humanity was desperately trying to beat into submission. And
inside the dome, humanity was doing the exact same thing to itself.
“Jax?” Chen’s voice broke her reverie.
“What?”
“I was running the property records on the penthouse. Tyrell doesn’t
own it.”
Jax turned around, raising an eyebrow. “At his salary? Why rent?”
“He isn’t renting,” Chen said, tapping her datapad, a frown creasing
her forehead. “The property is a corporate lease. It’s owned directly by
a shell corporation, which is a subsidiary of Vance Interplanetary.”
Jax sighed, feeling the familiar, heavy ache of a headache forming
behind her eyes. In Ares Prime, dealing with corporate murders meant
dealing with private security, suppressed evidence, and politicians
breathing down her neck. “So the company owns the victim, and the
company owns the crime scene.”
“Not just the company,” Chen corrected, looking up. She swallowed
hard. “The deed is registered in a personal trust. It belongs to Elara

Vance.”
The headache suddenly bloomed into a sharp spike. Elara Vance. The
CEO. The heir to the terraforming empire. The woman who practically
held the atmospheric controls of the entire colony in her perfectly
manicured hands.
Jax felt the familiar weight settle over her shoulders, the bone-deep
exhaustion that came with knowing a case was about to become a
nightmare. She’d worked corporate homicides before. They were never
clean. The moment a victim had ties to one of the Big Three—Vance
Interplanetary, Helios Terraforming, or Meridian Mining—the case
stopped being about justice and started being about damage control.
The lawyers would arrive within the hour, armed with injunctions
and gag orders. The Commissioner would get a call from someone with
enough political capital to make his career evaporate. Evidence would
disappear. Witnesses would recant. And Jax would be left holding a case
file full of redacted documents and dead ends, expected to close it quickly
and quietly without asking too many uncomfortable questions.
She’d seen it happen a dozen times. Hell, she’d been the one forced to
close those cases, filing reports that everyone knew were lies but no one
had the power—or the courage—to challenge.
But this was different. This wasn’t just corporate malfeasance or a
suspicious industrial accident. This was murder. Premeditated, precise,
and brutal. Someone had taken a Class-4 laser scalpel into Elara Vance’s
personal penthouse and dissected one of her senior executives like a lab
specimen.
That took resources. Access. And most importantly, it took a reason.
Jax’s mind was already running through the possibilities. Corporate
espionage gone wrong. A personal vendetta. A message sent to Vance
herself. Or maybe Tyrell had discovered something he wasn’t supposed
to know, and someone had decided to silence him permanently.
Whatever the reason, this case was going to be a fight. The kind of

fight that ended careers. The kind of fight that got good cops reassigned
to traffic duty in the outer mining sectors.
Jax had never been good at backing down.
“Great,” Jax muttered, running a hand through her dark, cropped hair.
“Lock down the elevators. Set up a perimeter. Nothing gets in or out
without my authorization.”
She took a breath, steadying herself. The crime scene was hers. For
now. She had maybe six hours before the corporate lawyers descended
like vultures, maybe twelve before the Commissioner pulled her off the
case entirely. She needed to work fast, document everything, and find
something—anything—that would give her leverage when the inevitable
pressure came.
The dead deserved that much. Tyrell deserved that much.
Even if he’d been a corporate executive living in a penthouse that cost
more than most people earned in a lifetime, he’d still been a person.
He’d still had a life, a story, a reason someone wanted him dead. And
Jax had learned a long time ago, buried in the darkness of Tantalus with
her parents’ bodies cooling beside her, that the dead couldn’t speak for
themselves.
Someone had to speak for them.
Before Chen could confirm the order, the mag-lev elevator at the end
of the hall chimed. A soft, melodic sound that signaled a direct override
of police lockdown protocols.
Jax unholstered her sidearm instinctively, dropping into a defensive
stance. “I said lock it down, Chen!”
“I did!” Chen panicked, frantically tapping her pad. “Someone
bypassed the precinct encryption. They have Level-1 Admin access.”
The elevator doors slid open with a whisper.
Jax leveled her weapon. “Ares Prime PD! Freeze!”
Footsteps echoed in the corridor, unhurried and deliberately rhythmic.
A silhouette stepped out of the shadows and into the stark lighting of

the foyer.
“You can put the gun away, Detective,” a smooth, dark voice echoed
through the room. “Unless you plan on shooting the woman who owns
the floor you’re standing on.”

title: Chapter Two: The Owner
pages:
Jax did not lower the weapon. Her stance remained wide, her grip
perfectly steady, the targeting laser of her sidearm painting a bright,
lethal red dot directly on the center of the intruder’s chest.
“I don’t care if you own the air we’re breathing,” Jax said, her voice
dropping to a gravelly register that usually made lower-level gang
runners scatter. “You just bypassed a Class-A police barricade. Identify
yourself, or you’ll be spending the night in a holding cell that doesn’t
care about your tax bracket.”
The woman stepped fully into the harsh, unsympathetic light of the
chandelier.
For a second—a long, suspended second that seemed to stretch
and distort like gravity itself—the sheer presence of her sucked the
remaining oxygen out of the room.
Jax had seen wealth before. She’d arrested trust-fund addicts in the
mid-levels, interrogated corporate executives who wore watches worth
more than her annual salary, even stood in the same room as colonial
senators draped in imported silk. But this was different. This wasn’t
just money. This was power, distilled into human form and wrapped in
a package designed to make lesser mortals forget how to breathe.

The woman was tall—easily six feet in the low-gravity environment
of the Zenith Domes—and she moved with a fluid, predatory grace that
suggested martial training hidden beneath layers of exorbitant wealth.
Every step was deliberate, controlled, economical. No wasted motion.
No hesitation. She wore a bespoke suit of charcoal grey that seemed to
absorb the light rather than reflect it. Not synth-weave, Jax noted with
a jolt of recognition that sent her pulse spiking, but actual, Earth-grown
wool, cut perfectly to the sharp lines of her shoulders and the narrow,
dangerous curve of her waist.
The tailoring was immaculate. The fabric moved like liquid shadow,
conforming to her body with the kind of precision that required a
personal tailor and measurements taken to the millimeter. The jacket
was open, revealing a crisp white shirt beneath, the collar unbuttoned
just enough to show the elegant line of her throat. No jewelry except
for a single silver cuff on her left wrist—understated, minimalist, and
probably worth more than Jax’s entire apartment building.
Her hair was a dark, liquid cascade swept cleanly back from her face,
highlighting high, aristocratic cheekbones and a jawline that could cut
glass. Her skin was pale, almost luminous in the artificial light, the kind
of complexion that came from never having to work under the harsh
Martian sun, never having to ration water for bathing, never having to
worry about the micro-abrasions that came from breathing recycled air
laced with red dust.
But it was her eyes that made the hairs on the back of Jax’s neck stand
up.
They were a startling, icy silver—not grey, not blue, but genuine metal-
lic silver, the kind that only came from high-end genetic modification
or neural-lace integration. And they were fixed entirely on Jax with an
intensity that felt almost physical, like a weight pressing against her
chest.
Jax’s finger tightened fractionally on the trigger. Her training

screamed at her to maintain the threat posture, to establish dominance,
to not let this woman—whoever she was—take control of the situation.
But something deeper, something primal and entirely unwelcome, was
making her heart hammer against her ribs in a rhythm that had nothing
to do with adrenaline and everything to do with the way those silver eyes
were cataloguing her, measuring her, seeing her in a way that made her
feel simultaneously exposed and electrified.
“Elara Vance,” the woman said. Her tone was a low, melodic drawl,
entirely unbothered by the heavy-caliber pistol pointed at her heart.
Each word was precisely enunciated, carrying the kind of confidence
that came from never having been told ‘no’ in her entire life. “And the
air you’re breathing right now? I do, in fact, own it. You’re welcome.”
Behind Jax, Officer Chen made a sound that was half-gasp, half-choke.
“Sir—Detective—that’s the CEO.”
“I know who she is, Chen,” Jax snapped, never breaking eye contact
with the silver-eyed billionaire. Her mind was racing, cataloguing
details, running threat assessments, trying desperately to ignore the
way her pulse was doing complicated, unwelcome things in her throat.
Reluctantly, feeling the absolute wrongness of it in her gut, Jax flicked
the safety on and holstered her weapon. “You’re contaminating my
crime scene, Ms. Vance.”
“It’s my property, Detective. I’m simply inspecting the damage.”
Elara walked past Jax, leaving a slipstream of air in her wake.
Jax caught the scent and her brain misfired for a fraction of a second. It
wasn’t the synthetic, chemical perfumes peddled in the mid-levels, the
kind that smelled like industrial solvents and false promises. It wasn’t
the cheap, floral body sprays that lower-sector workers used to mask
the smell of recycled sweat. It smelled like crushed damp leaves, old
paper, and a faint, electric hint of ozone. It smelled like a thunderstorm
on Earth—a planet Jax had never visited, would never be able to afford
to visit, but had dreamed about in the darkness of her childhood. It was

an intoxicating, impossible scent on a dead planet.
Jax shook her head, forcing her focus back to the corpse as Elara
stopped at the edge of the Persian rug, looking down at her murdered
Vice President of Atmospheric Engineering.
For a long moment, Elara simply stood there, perfectly still. The
classical symphony was still playing softly in the background—Bach,
Jax realized now, something baroque and mathematical and entirely
inappropriate for a murder scene. The holographic fireplace crackled
with simulated flames. The smart-glass windows reflected the distant,
neon-scarred sprawl of the lower colony.
And Elara Vance stared down at the dissected corpse of Marcus Tyrell
with an expression that Jax couldn’t quite read.
Most civilians—even the hardened ones—flinched when they saw a
Class-4 laser dissection. The charred flesh, the exposed, calcified ribs,
the sheer violence of it usually elicited a visceral reaction. Jax had seen
corporate executives vomit at crime scenes. She’d seen gang bosses turn
pale. She’d seen hardened soldiers look away.
Elara Vance didn’t blink.
Her silver eyes tracked over the body with cool, terrifying clinical
detachment. She tilted her head slightly, studying the pattern of the
cuts, the precision of the incisions, the cauterization marks along the
edges of the wounds. Her expression didn’t change. No horror. No
disgust. No shock. Just… assessment.
Jax watched her carefully, reading the micro-expressions, the subtle
shifts in body language that most people didn’t even realize they were
broadcasting. Elara’s breathing was steady, controlled. Her hands were
relaxed at her sides, not clenched, not trembling. Her pupils were slightly
dilated, but that could have been the lighting. There was no tension in
her shoulders, no tightness around her mouth.
Either she was a sociopath, or she’d seen violence before. Real violence.
The kind that left scars.

Jax filed that information away for later.
“Messy,” Elara concluded, her voice barely a murmur. “Tyrell was a
fastidious man. He would have hated this.”
“I imagine the being dead part is what he hates the most,” Jax said
dryly, stepping up beside her. Not too close. Just close enough to re-
establish her territory, to remind Elara Vance that this was still a police
investigation, still Jax’s jurisdiction, no matter how many billions of
credits were standing next to her. “How did you know he was dead? My
precinct hasn’t even notified his next of kin.”
Elara finally turned her head, looking at Jax. She was close enough
now that Jax could see the faint, metallic rim of neural-lace implants
tracing her left temple—high-end cognitive enhancers, the kind that
cost more than a house in the mid-levels. The kind that could process
data streams in real-time, interface directly with quantum computing
networks, and probably hack into most civilian security systems without
breaking a sweat.
“Tyrell was wearing a Vance Interplanetary biometric monitor,” Elara
said, her gaze dropping to Jax’s mouth for a micro-second before
returning to her eyes. The movement was so quick, so subtle, that
Jax almost convinced herself she’d imagined it. Almost. “When his
heart stopped, the data spiked to my personal security grid before the
automated ping ever reached your archaic police dispatch. I happened
to be in the adjoining sector.”
“Convenient,” Jax muttered, her detective’s instincts screaming at
her to dig deeper, to question the timing, to not accept anything at face
value.
“Efficiency usually is,” Elara countered smoothly. “The biometric
monitors are standard issue for all senior executives. They track heart
rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation, neural activity, and a dozen
other vital signs in real-time. The data is encrypted and transmitted
via quantum-entangled relay to our central medical AI. When Tyrell’s

vitals flatlined at 0342 hours, the system flagged it as a critical medical
emergency and sent an immediate alert to my personal grid, my Chief of
Security, and the on-call medical team.”
“And you just happened to be close enough to arrive before the police,”
Jax said, not bothering to hide the skepticism in her voice.
“I was in a board meeting in the adjoining tower,” Elara replied, her
tone perfectly neutral. “I excused myself the moment I received the alert.
My private transport can make the cross-dome transit in under three
minutes.”
Jax filed that away too. Three minutes. Fast enough to be the first on
scene. Fast enough to tamper with evidence, if she’d wanted to.
“And you are?” Elara asked, tilting her head a fraction of an inch, a
ghost of a smile playing on her lips.
“Detective Jax. Homicide.”
Elara’s smile widened, just slightly. “Just Jax? Like a stray dog?”
Jax felt a flare of irritation, hot and sharp. “Like a cop who wants to
know why the CEO of the biggest conglomerate on Mars is rushing to a
crime scene instead of sending her lawyers,” Jax shot back, crossing her
arms over her chest. The worn synth-leather of her coat creaked, a harsh,
discordant sound in the pristine silence of the penthouse. “Usually,
people like you do everything in your power to stay as far away from a
bleeding corpse as possible.”
“People like me,” Elara repeated, her voice dropping to a lower
register, something darker and more dangerous threading through the
words. “You mean wealthy people, Detective? Or do you mean people
who actually have something to lose?”
“I mean people who can afford to make problems disappear,” Jax said
flatly.
“Ah.” Elara’s silver eyes glittered with something that might have
been amusement or might have been anger. It was impossible to tell.
“You think I’m here to cover something up. How delightfully cynical.”

“I’m a homicide detective in Ares Prime,” Jax said. “Cynicism is a job
requirement.”
“Fair enough.” Elara took a step closer, closing the distance between
them until Jax could feel the heat radiating from her body, could see the
faint pulse beating in her throat. “But let me be very clear, Detective
Jax. Tyrell wasn’t just an employee. He possessed intimate, heavily
classified knowledge of the colony’s atmospheric regulators. He knew
the structural tolerances of every dome, the redundancy protocols, the
emergency shutdown sequences. He had access to systems that, if
compromised, could kill thousands of people in a matter of hours.”
Elara’s gaze hardened, the icy silver flashing with genuine steel. “If
someone tortured him before they killed him—and judging by the
cauterization patterns, they did—I need to know what he told them.
I need to know if my infrastructure is compromised. I need to know if
there’s a bomb waiting to go off somewhere in my colony.”
Jax felt a flare of irritation. “This is a homicide investigation, Ms.
Vance. Not a corporate espionage audit. You don’t get to run parallel
inquiries.”
“I am not asking for your permission,” Elara said softly. It wasn’t a
threat; it was a simple statement of fact, delivered with the crushing
weight of a woman who bought and sold senators before breakfast.
Jax took a step closer, invading Elara’s personal space. She had to tilt
her head up slightly to meet the billionaire’s eyes, but she didn’t yield
an inch of ground. “Listen to me very carefully. I don’t care how many
credits you have in your accounts. You are standing in my jurisdiction.
This body is my case. And if you interfere, I will arrest you for obstruction,
and I will thoroughly enjoy watching you sit in a holding cell that smells
like stale urine and regret.”
Silence stretched between them, heavy and electric. The air felt
charged, like the moment before a lightning strike. Chen was standing
frozen by the window, desperately pretending to be invisible, her datapad

clutched to her chest like a shield.
Jax waited for the explosion. She waited for the threats, the demands
to speak to her captain, the imperious rage of the elite. She’d seen it
a hundred times before—wealthy suspects who couldn’t comprehend
that their money didn’t buy them immunity from the law.
Instead, Elara Vance smiled. It was a slow, predatory curving of her
lips that made Jax’s stomach do a complicated, unwelcome flip.
“You’re very fiercely protective of your territory, Detective,” Elara
murmured, her voice dropping to an intimate frequency that seemed
meant only for Jax. “I can appreciate that.”
Jax swallowed hard, determined not to let the flush creeping up her
neck reach her face. “Step back from the body, Ms. Vance.”
Elara held her hands up in a gesture of mock surrender and took a slow
step backward. “Of course. I wouldn’t dream of stepping on your toes.
In fact, I’m going to make your life considerably easier.”
Elara tapped the silver cuff linking her left wrist. A small, holographic
data-chit materialized in the air between them, glowing with soft blue
light. “Your police grid is a joke, Jax. It’ll take your technicians three days
just to decrypt the building’s lobby cameras. Here is the raw, unedited
feed from my private security network for the last forty-eight hours,
covering the entire Zenith dome perimeter and this penthouse.”
Jax stared at the glowing blue data-chit. It was exactly what she needed.
It was also a Trojan horse.
“Why give me this?” Jax asked, her voice carefully neutral.
“Because whoever killed Tyrell bypassed three layers of my proprietary
encryption to do it,” Elara said, the amusement finally vanishing from
her face, leaving behind a cold, calculating intellect. “They made me
look vulnerable. I want them found. You want them found. Our goals
align.”
“For now,” Jax said. She reached out and snatched the data-chit from
the air. The momentary brush of her gloved fingers against Elara’s bare

skin sent a bizarre, static shock racing up Jax’s arm.
Elara’s eyes tracked the movement. “I expect to be kept in the loop on
your findings, Detective.”
“You’ll get the public briefing like everyone else,” Jax said, slipping
the chit into her pocket.
“We’ll see,” Elara replied quietly. She turned to leave, the heavy,
expensive fabric of her coat sweeping around her ankles. She paused at
the threshold of the mag-lev elevator and looked back over her shoulder.
“It was a pleasure meeting you, Jax. I have a feeling we’re going to be
seeing a great deal of each other.”
The elevator doors slid shut, cutting off the view of her sharp smile.
Jax stood in the silence of the room for a long moment, the scent
of the Earth thunderstorm still lingering in her lungs. Her pulse was
hammering against her ribs, a heavy, adrenaline-fueled rhythm that
had nothing to do with the dead body on the floor.
Her mind was racing, cataloguing everything that had just happened,
running threat assessments, trying to piece together the implications.
Elara Vance had given her the security footage. Just handed it over, no
lawyers, no negotiations, no demands for reciprocity. That was either
a gesture of good faith or a calculated move to control the narrative.
Probably both.
The data-chit felt heavy in her pocket, like a live grenade. It would give
her access to Vance’s private security network, which meant she’d be
able to see exactly who came and went from the penthouse in the forty-
eight hours before Tyrell’s death. But it also meant Elara would know
exactly what Jax was looking at, when she was looking at it, and what
conclusions she was drawing. The woman had neural-lace implants.
She could probably monitor the data access in real-time.
Jax didn’t trust it. She didn’t trust Elara Vance. And she especially
didn’t trust the way her body had reacted to the woman’s proximity—
the spike in her pulse, the heat in her chest, the way her skin had felt

electrified when their fingers had brushed.
That was dangerous. That was the kind of distraction that got good
cops killed.
But she couldn’t deny the woman was right about one thing: their
goals did align. Jax wanted to find Tyrell’s killer. Elara wanted to protect
her infrastructure. And if someone had tortured a senior executive for
classified information about the colony’s atmospheric systems, then this
case was bigger than a simple homicide. This was terrorism. Sabotage.
Maybe even the prelude to a mass-casualty event.
Jax exhaled slowly, rubbing the bridge of her nose. The headache
was back, sharper than before, pulsing behind her eyes in time with her
heartbeat.
She needed to get back to the precinct. She needed to run the security
footage through forensic analysis. She needed to dig into Tyrell’s
background, his communications, his financial records. She needed
to figure out who had the skills, the access, and the motive to bypass
three layers of Vance’s proprietary encryption and dissect a man with a
Class-4 laser scalpel.
And she needed to do it before Elara Vance decided to take matters into
her own hands.
“Detective?” Chen ventured nervously from the corner, her voice
small and uncertain. “Are you okay?”
Jax turned to look at her junior officer. Chen’s eyes were wide, her face
pale, her datapad still clutched to her chest like a lifeline. She looked
like she’d just witnessed something she didn’t have the vocabulary to
process.
Jax took a breath, forcing herself back into the familiar armor of
professionalism. She straightened her shoulders, rolled her neck to
release the tension, and let the mask of the hardened detective slide back
into place.
“Yeah, Chen,” Jax muttered, turning her back to the elevator and

staring down at the grisly remains of Marcus Tyrell. “I’m fine. But notify
the Captain. Vance Interplanetary is officially involved, and I think the
CEO just decided to make this case her personal entertainment.”
She looked at the data-chit glowing through the fabric of her coat
pocket.
“Come on,” Jax said, her voice turning hard and entirely professional
once more. “Let’s bag this up. We’re going to be working late.”

title: Chapter Three: The Bureaucracy
pages:
Precinct 12, Sector Four, smelled like stale sweat, burnt ozone, and the
sour tang of cheap synth-caf. It was a sensory assault that Jax usually
found grounding, but after the sterile, pine-scented wealth of the Zenith
Domes, it just felt suffocating.
The precinct was a subterranean concrete bunker buried deep beneath
the colony’s surface, holding the line between the struggling mid-levels
and the lawless mining sectors. The atmospheric scrubbers down here
had been rattling for a decade, pumping out air that always tasted faintly
of old pennies.
Jax bypassed her desk entirely and headed straight for the glass-walled
office at the back of the bullpen. She didn’t bother knocking.
Captain Graves looked up from his terminal, his face illuminated by the
harsh, flickering glow of the screen. He was a man who looked exactly
like the sector he policed: battered, permanently exhausted, and held
together by cheap nicotine patches and spite.
“I haven’t even had my morning ration, Jax,” Graves grunted, rubbing

a hand over his deeply lined face. “Tell me you didn’t piss off the single
most powerful woman on this rock.”
“Good morning to you too, Captain,” Jax said, dropping into the
uncomfortable poly-plas chair across from his desk. It squeaked in
protest. “And for the record, I merely secured my crime scene. If Elara
Vance felt insulted that I didn’t roll out the red carpet over a brutally
murdered corpse, that’s her problem.”
Graves closed his eyes, inhaling a long, slow breath through his nose.
“Vance’s legal team has already called the Commissioner, who then
called the Chief, who then called me. They want this wrapped up, Jax.
Neatly. Quietly. Yesterday.”
“A Vance Interplanetary executive got fileted with a restricted military
laser in a secure penthouse,” Jax said, her voice tightening. “There is no
‘neat’ and ‘quiet’ here, Graves. The killer bypassed a fortune in corporate
security to do it.”
“Exactly,” Graves snapped, leaning forward. “Which is why Vance
thinks it’s corporate espionage. A rival firm sending a message. She
wants her private security handling the fallout, and she wants us to
stamp a ‘robbery gone wrong’ on the file and walk away.”
Jax let out a harsh, barking laugh. “A robbery? Nothing was taken!
The guy’s antique Earth-art collection was untouched. This was an
execution, and Vance knows it. She gave me her private security feeds.”
Graves paused, his eyebrows shooting up. “Vance handed over her
private network data? Willingly?”
“Handed it to me herself,” Jax said, pulling the blue, glowing data-chit
from her pocket and tossing it onto Graves’s desk. It clattered against
his cheap plastic stylus. “She wants to find the killer just as much as
we do. But she wants to do it on her terms, which means she’s hiding
something.”
Graves stared at the chit like it was a live thermal detonator. “Jax,
listen to me. You dig too deep into Vance Interplanetary, you’re not just

risking your badge. You’re risking your oxygen ration. These people
don’t play by the same laws we enforce down here.”
“I don’t care how much air she owns,” Jax said, her jaw setting into a
stubborn line. “Tyrell is my body now. I speak for him. And I am going
to find out who turned his chest cavity into a puzzle.”
Graves studied her for a long moment, reading the immutable resolve
in her dark eyes. He sighed, a sound of utter defeat. “Seventy-two
hours, Jax. You have three days to find me a viable suspect before the
Commissioner forces me to hand jurisdiction over to Vance’s private
army. Keep your head down. And for the love of God, don’t antagonize
the woman again.”
“I’ll be the picture of bureaucratic compliance,” Jax lied smoothly,
standing up.
“I mean it, Jax!” Graves yelled as she walked out.
Jax let the glass door click shut behind her, immediately tuning out
the chaotic noise of the bullpen. Seventy-two hours. Three days to solve
a murder that had corporate fingerprints all over it, or watch the case get
buried under a mountain of legal injunctions and classified redactions.
It was the same song and dance she’d seen a dozen times before. The
wealthy killed each other with impunity, and the cops in the lower sectors
were expected to look the other way and be grateful for the privilege.
She walked slowly through the precinct, her boots echoing on the
cracked polymer flooring. Around her, the machinery of colonial law
enforcement ground forward with exhausted inevitability. Officer
Ramirez was processing a drunk-and-disorderly from the transit hub,
the suspect still reeking of industrial solvent and bad decisions. Detective
Kowalski was on the phone with a domestic violence victim who was
already recanting her statement, her voice small and defeated through
the speaker. The holding cells in the back were full of the usual suspects—
water thieves, ration forgers, unlicensed oxygen dealers.
This was the reality of police work on Mars. They didn’t solve crimes;

they managed the chaos. They kept the desperate from killing each other
over scraps while the people in the Zenith Domes lived like gods and
pretended the lower sectors didn’t exist. Jax had made peace with that
reality a long time ago. She couldn’t fix the system. She couldn’t make
Mars fair. But she could speak for the dead, and that had to be enough.
Even when the dead were corporate executives who’d lived in pent-
houses worth more than her entire precinct’s annual budget.
She walked over to her desk—a scarred metal slab piled high with
flimsies and half-empty water pouches.
Chen was already there, aggressively tapping away at her dual-screen
setup. Her uniform was impossibly crisp, a stark contrast to the
organized disaster of Jax’s workspace.
“Tell me you have something, Chen,” Jax said, dropping into her
chair and kicking her boots up onto the edge of the desk. She rubbed
her temples. The headache was migrating from a dull ache to a sharp
stabbing sensation behind her left eye.
“I’m running the data-chit Vance gave us through our decryption
algorithms,” Chen said, not looking away from the cascading lines of
code on her screen. “It’s clean. No viruses, no backdoors. It’s exactly
what she said it was—forty-eight hours of raw, uncompressed video
and sensory data.”
“Any sign of our phantom surgeon on the feeds?”
“Nothing so far. The guy was a ghost. But,” Chen hit a keystroke with
a triumphant flourish, “I did pull Tyrell’s personal comms logs before
Vance’s corporate goons could scrub them. And things get interesting.”
Jax leaned forward, the headache momentarily forgotten. “Show me.”
“Tyrell was the VP of Atmospheric Engineering, which means he
oversaw the pressure regulators for the lower sectors,” Chen explained,
bringing up a complex schematic of the colony’s dome structure. “For
the last three weeks, he’s been making encrypted, off-the-grid calls to
a burner comm in Sector 6. The mining districts.”

“Why is a Zenith Dome executive talking to rock-crushers in Sector
6?”
“That’s the million-credit question,” Chen said. “I dug into Tyrell’s
personal file partition. It was heavily encrypted, but whoever killed him
left a backdoor wide open when they wiped his main drive. Tyrell was
compiling data on Sector 4’s water recycling plants. Specifically, the
filtration quotas.”
Jax frowned, staring at the numbers scrolling across the screen. Sector
4 was right above them. It was a massive, densely populated district
of factory workers and low-level corporate drones. “The quotas look
normal.”
“On paper, yes,” Chen said. “But look at the actual output logs Tyrell
buried in this hidden folder.”
Chen overlaid a second set of numbers. Jax wasn’t a mathematician,
but she didn’t need to be to see the glaring discrepancy.
“The output is short,” Jax murmured. “They’re siphoning the water.”
“Millions of gallons of it over the last six months,” Chen confirmed,
her fingers dancing across the keyboard to pull up additional data
streams. “Someone is systematically diverting purified water meant for
the lower sectors and doctoring the ledgers to cover it up. Tyrell found
out.”
Jax studied the numbers more carefully, her detective’s mind already
running scenarios. “Walk me through the technical side. How would
someone even pull this off without triggering alarms?”
Chen pulled up a schematic of the filtration plant’s control systems.
“The water recycling plants are automated. They’re monitored by AI
systems that track input, filtration efficiency, and output distribution.
But there’s a manual override protocol for maintenance and emergency
repairs. If someone had administrative access to the plant’s control
network, they could redirect water flow during scheduled maintenance
windows and alter the output logs to match the expected quotas.”

“So we’re looking for someone with high-level system access,” Jax
said. “Plant managers, senior engineers, maybe corporate oversight.”
“Exactly. And here’s where it gets worse.” Chen brought up a financial
analysis overlay. “I cross-referenced the water theft timeline with
commodity pricing in the outer sectors. During the same six-month
period, black-market water prices in Sectors 5 and 6 dropped by thirty
percent. Someone was flooding the illegal market with stolen water.”
“Water is more valuable than currency down here,” Jax said, her mind
racing, piecing the puzzle together. “If Tyrell was getting ready to blow
the whistle on a massive water-theft ring, that’s a hell of a motive for
murder. But who was he talking to in Sector 6?”
“I’m still trying to trace the burner comm,” Chen said, pulling
up a network map showing the signal’s path through the colony’s
communication infrastructure. “It’s bouncing off a dozen different
relay towers. Whoever set it up knew what they were doing. But I’m
running a pattern analysis on the call timestamps and locations. If I can
narrow down the physical location of the burner comm when the calls
were made, I might be able to identify the recipient.”
“How long?”
“Give me six hours. Maybe less if I can get access to the Sector 6 transit
authority’s surveillance feeds.”
Jax nodded, her mind already working through the implications. A
water-theft conspiracy involving millions of gallons meant serious
money. It meant organized crime, probably cartel involvement. And if
Tyrell had been negotiating with someone in Sector 6, he’d either been
trying to expose them or cut himself in on the profits. Either way, he’d
made himself a target.
“Keep on it,” Jax ordered. “And look into Vance Interplanetary’s water
distribution subsidiaries. I want to know who signs the checks for the
filtration plants.”
“On it, boss.”

“Package for Detective Jax!”
Jax looked up to see the precinct’s desk sergeant, an older, heavy-set
man named Miller, walking toward her desk. He was carrying a sleek,
matte-black cylindrical container that looked like it belonged on a luxury
starship, not in a grimy police bullpen.
“I didn’t order anything,” Jax said, eyeing the cylinder suspiciously.
“Courier dropped it off three minutes ago,” Miller said, setting it down
on her desk with excessive care. “Said it was priority cleared by the
Commissioner’s office. Fancy drone, too. Had the Vance Interplanetary
logo on the side.”
Chen stopped typing, her eyes going wide.
Jax stared at the cylinder. It was about ten inches tall, made of some
heavy, temperature-regulating thermal-alloy. Not the cheap polymer
containers used for standard deliveries, but actual machined metal with
seamless welds and a mirror-polished finish. There was a small, heavy-
stock paper envelope tucked beneath a silver clip on the side.
Actual paper.
Around the bullpen, other officers were starting to notice. Ramirez had
stopped mid-booking to stare. Kowalski had ended his phone call and
was craning his neck to get a better look. Even the suspects in the holding
cells were pressing against the bars, their eyes fixed on the impossibly
expensive container sitting on Jax’s desk.
In a colony where everything was recycled, reused, and rationed, the
cylinder was an obscenity. It was a physical manifestation of wealth so
extreme it might as well have been radioactive.
Jax pulled the envelope loose. Her name, \*Detective Jax\*, was
written across the front in an elegant, flowing script in dark blue ink.
Not printed. Handwritten.
She broke the wax seal—an actual wax seal, for God’s sake—and
unfolded the thick parchment.
*Detective,*

*The air in Sector Four is notoriously poor, and I imagine your
precinct’s coffee is akin to drinking battery acid. You strike me as a
woman who requires proper fuel to operate at maximum efficiency.*
*Consider this an investment in our mutual goal. For the headache.*
*- E.*
Jax read the note once. Then twice. Then a third time, her jaw
tightening with each pass.
The sheer, unadulterated arrogance of it was staggering. Elara Vance
was tracking her. She knew Jax was assigned to Sector Four, she had
somehow guessed (or observed) Jax’s caffeine dependency, and she
was flexing her impossible wealth by sending a handwritten note on
real wood-pulp paper. The note itself was a power play—intimate,
presumptuous, and designed to remind Jax exactly who held the cards
in their relationship.
*For the headache.*\*\*\*\* As if Elara Vance wasn’t the primary
cause of the headache currently drilling into Jax’s skull.
This was manipulation. Pure, calculated manipulation wrapped in a
veneer of thoughtfulness. Elara was establishing control, demonstrating
that she could reach into Jax’s life whenever she wanted, that she was
watching, that she knew things about Jax that Jax hadn’t told her. It was
invasive. It was inappropriate. It was a blatant attempt to buy influence
over a police investigation.
Jax should report it. She should document it as attempted bribery and
send it straight to Internal Affairs. She should throw the entire package
in the incinerator and pretend it never arrived.
But her fingers were already reaching for the silver cap of the cylinder.
“What is it?” Chen whispered, leaning over her desk.
Jax didn’t answer. She reached out and twisted the silver cap of the
cylinder.
There was a soft *hiss*\*\*\*\*\*\* of depressurization.\*\*
Instantly, the scent hit the air. It rolled over the smell of stale sweat and

ozone like a tidal wave. It was rich, dark, earthy, and so incredibly potent
that conversations in the bullpen literally ground to a halt. Officers three
desks away turned their heads, their nostrils flaring.
It was real, Earth-grown, freshly ground and brewed coffee.
In a colony where ninety-nine percent of the population drank syn-
thetic, soy-based caffeine substitutes, the smell of real coffee was a
mythological experience. It was the scent of pure, distilled luxury. It
was the smell of a planet most of them would never see, a world of blue
skies and liquid oceans and air that didn’t taste like recycled metal.
The bullpen had gone completely silent. Every officer, every suspect,
every person in the room was staring at Jax’s desk with expressions
ranging from awe to naked hunger to barely concealed resentment.
Ramirez took an involuntary step forward, his eyes fixed on the
cylinder. “Is that…?”
“Holy mother of Mars,” Miller whispered, staring at the thermos. “Is
that…?”
Kowalski had actually stood up from his desk, his mouth slightly open.
“That’s real coffee. That’s actual, Earth-grown coffee.”
The suspect Ramirez had been booking—a scrawny water thief with
track marks on his arms—pressed his face against the bars of the holding
cell. “I can smell it from here,” he said, his voice cracking. “I haven’t
smelled real coffee in fifteen years.”
The moment crystallized into something ugly. The cylinder sitting on
Jax’s desk wasn’t just a gift. It was a symbol. It was a reminder of the
vast, unbridgeable chasm between the people who lived in the Zenith
Domes and everyone else. It was a reminder that while they scraped
by on recycled water and synthetic food, people like Elara Vance could
casually send Earth-grown coffee to a cop as a throwaway gesture.
It was class warfare, distilled into a single, perfect container.
“Go back to your desk, Miller,” Jax snapped, quickly screwing the cap
back on before she caused a riot in the bullpen.

Her hand was actually shaking slightly. The headache behind her eye
throbbed, demanding the caffeine. She hated the presumption. She
hated that Elara Vance thought she could buy her compliance, or even
her goodwill, with a thermos of hot liquid. It was a power play. A velvet-
wrapped assertion of dominance.
But beneath the anger, beneath the professional outrage, there was
something else. Something she didn’t want to acknowledge. Elara had
noticed. She’d noticed Jax’s headache, noticed her exhaustion, noticed
the way she’d been rubbing her temples in the penthouse. And instead
of ignoring it or using it as leverage, she’d sent something to help.
It was thoughtful. Genuinely, infuriatingly thoughtful.
And that made it so much worse.
Jax stared at the matte-black container. She should throw it in the
incinerator. She should send it back with a citation for attempting to
bribe a police officer. She should maintain her professional boundaries
and refuse to accept gifts from suspects or persons of interest in an active
investigation.
All of those were the right decisions. The ethical decisions. The
decisions a good cop would make.
But Jax was exhausted. She’d been running on three hours of sleep
and synthetic caffeine for the last week. Her head was pounding. Her
body ached from the tension of standing in Elara Vance’s presence and
pretending she wasn’t affected. And the smell of that coffee—real, Earth-
grown coffee—was making her mouth water and her resolve crumble.
She told herself it was evidence. She told herself she needed to test it
for contaminants or tracking devices. She told herself a dozen different
lies that sounded almost plausible.
Slowly, deliberately, Jax unscrewed the cap again. She poured a
measure of the steaming, pitch-black liquid into the small silver cup
attached to the lid.
“Jax,” Chen said, her voice a mix of awe and warning. “If Captain

Graves sees you drinking that…”
“I’m securing evidence, Chen,” Jax muttered.
She lifted the cup to her lips and took a sip.
It was a revelation. It was smooth, devoid of the bitter chemical
aftertaste of synth-caf, blooming with notes of dark chocolate and actual
soil. It slid down her throat like liquid fire, instantly sending a shockwave
of clarity through her exhausted brain. The headache didn’t stand a
chance.
Jax closed her eyes for a brief, shameful second, savoring the taste.
*Damn her,*\*\*\*\* Jax thought, taking another deep drink. *Damn
Elara Vance.*
She set the cup down with a sharp *clack*\*\*\*\*\*\* against the
metal desk, the caffeine already sharpening her edge. She looked back
at the schematics on Chen’s monitor.\*\*
“Alright,” Jax said, her voice vibrating with renewed, wired energy.
“Vance wants to play games, let’s play. Pull the personnel files for every
manager at the Sector 4 filtration plant. If someone is stealing water,
they’re doing it from the ground up. I want to know who’s getting rich
down here.”
As Chen’s fingers flew across the keyboard, Jax took another sip of
the billion-credit coffee, the handwritten note from Elara Vance sitting
squarely on her desk like a challenge she couldn’t wait to answer.
